title: Split Array with Minimum Largest Sum

content.problemStatement:
You are given an array of integers numsnumsnums of size NNN and an integer KKK. Your task is to split the array into **exactly K non-empty contiguous subarrays** such that the **largest sum** among these subarrays is minimized.

Return the minimized largest sum.

Function DeclarationFunction NamesplitArraysplitArraysplitArray – This function splits an array into KKK contiguous subarrays and minimizes the maximum subarray sum.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : An array of non-negative integers
KKK : The number of non-empty contiguous subarrays to split the array into
Return ValueReturns a single integer — the **minimum possible value of the largest subarray sum** after splitting

Constraints
1≤N≤10001 \leq N \leq 10001≤N≤1000
0≤nums[i]≤1060 \leq nums[i] \leq 10^60≤nums[i]≤106
1≤K≤min(50,N)1 \leq K \leq min(50, N)1≤K≤min(50,N)
The array must be split into **exactly K subarrays**
Each subarray must be **non-empty**
The input and output formats provided below are only for testing with custom inputs. You only need to complete the core logic function.


Input Format

The first line contains two integers NNN and KKK — the size of the array and the number of subarrays
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers nums[0],nums[1],...,nums[N−1]nums[0], nums[1], ..., nums[N-1]nums[0],nums[1],...,nums[N−1] — the elements of the array


Output Format
Print a single integer: the **minimum possible value of the largest sum** among the KKK subarrays
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and K — the size of the array and the required number of subarrays. The second line contains N space‑separated non‑negative integers representing the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible value of the largest sum among the K contiguous subarrays.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First case: optimal split [1,4,4] [2,6] [3] gives max sum 9. Second case: split [2,3,5] [7,1,4] [2] [6] gives max 10. Third case: split after first three elements gives sums 6 and 9, max 9.
All elements are zero, any split yields max sum 0.
Each element must be its own subarray, so the largest sum equals the maximum element 5.

solution.approach: We binary‑search the answer X (the maximal subarray sum). The lower bound is the largest single element, the upper bound is the total sum. For a candidate X we greedily scan the array, accumulating a running sum. When adding the next element would exceed X we start a new subarray and increase the count. If we need more than K subarrays the candidate X is too small; otherwise it is feasible. The smallest feasible X is the answer. This runs in O(N log S) per test case where S is the sum of all elements.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log (sum of array)) per test case
